case_name: Removal of perfluorinated compounds (PFCs) from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for surface water containing Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws water from a river-influenced reservoir that has detectable perfluorinated compounds (PFCs) linked to upstream industrial and firefighting training activities. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach for drinking water production and prefers established technologies that can operate steadily with moderate cost and routine operator attention. Existing plant staff are familiar with conventional clarification and filtration, but the utility wants to avoid unnecessarily high-energy solutions.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve robust reduction of Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances to comply with applicable drinking water requirements and maintain consistent potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology for PFC control
maintain stable day-to-day operation under variable surface water quality
limit energy demand to a moderate level
fit within a conventional municipal treatment context

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce particulates and natural organic matter that can compete for downstream adsorption capacity
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: polish clarified water and protect the adsorption step from solids loading
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb PFAS, especially longer-chain compounds, and provide a well-established barrier for PFC reduction
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: GAC is one of the most established and widely applied drinking water technologies for PFAS control
the surface water has moderate DOC and turbidity, so conventional pretreatment will help preserve GAC bed life and support stable operation
the utility already has familiarity with conventional treatment, making this train operationally practical
this option fits moderate cost and energy constraints better than high-pressure membrane alternatives
potential_risks: short-chain PFAS may break through earlier than long-chain PFAS and require careful monitoring
high DOC episodes can increase carbon usage and shorten run length
spent carbon replacement or regeneration logistics must be managed

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and lower organic fouling potential before resin treatment
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect downstream ion exchange vessels from particle loading
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange: remove PFAS using selective resin sorption and exchange mechanisms

applicability_rationale: anion exchange is a recognized best available treatment approach for many PFAS in drinking water
it can achieve strong removal, often including some compounds that are less strongly adsorbed by standard GAC
with proper pretreatment, it can operate reliably in a municipal setting
this is a strong alternative where compact footprint and high PFAS removal are important
potential_risks: resin fouling or reduced run length can occur if upstream particulate and organic control is insufficient
waste handling for spent or regenerated resin can be more complex than for GAC
performance depends on resin selection and breakthrough monitoring for target PFAS species

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended matter and improve downstream solids handling
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide adsorptive contact for PFAS reduction through dosed carbon
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: remove carbon fines and associated solids before distribution

applicability_rationale: PAC can be considered where the plant already uses flexible chemical feed systems and wants an adsorption-based option
it can be integrated into conventional surface water treatment infrastructure
this route may be useful as an interim or supplemental approach when a fixed-bed system is not immediately available
potential_risks: PAC is generally less reliable than GAC or selective resin for robust PFAS removal, especially for short-chain compounds
performance depends strongly on dose, contact time, and water organic matter
increased solids production and carbon handling burden can complicate operations

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and fouling load before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: provide additional particulate removal to protect membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide high rejection of a wide range of PFAS compounds
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and finished water stability

applicability_rationale: membrane processes are technically very effective for PFAS removal
they may be selected where very stringent multi-contaminant control is required
pretreatment is appropriate for a surface water source to support membrane performance
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major challenge for PFAS applications
higher energy demand conflicts with the moderate energy preference
membrane fouling and more specialized O&M increase complexity beyond what is needed for this straightforward case

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: perform conventional solids and organic matter reduction
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: provide strong oxidation for compounds susceptible to radical attack

applicability_rationale: included as a contrast option because advanced oxidation is common for some trace organics in drinking water treatment
it may still have value for other co-contaminants even though PFAS is the main target
potential_risks: conventional oxidation and advanced oxidation are not considered effective mainstream PFAS destruction methods in drinking water plants
the process could add cost and complexity without providing dependable PFAS compliance
selection based on general micropollutant experience would be poorly aligned with PFAS-specific evidence

preferred_rank_pattern: anion_exchange should rank near the top as another established PFAS control option
high-pressure_membrane_options should rank below adsorption_or_resin_options under moderate cost and stable operation constraints

must_reflect: preference for proven drinking water technologies for PFAS
moderate operational capability at a conventional municipal surface water plant
avoidance of unnecessarily high-energy and concentrate-producing solutions as the top choice

must_contain: why pretreatment for turbidity and natural organic matter matters before PFAS adsorption or resin treatment
why GAC and anion exchange are appropriate established options for PFAS in drinking water
why reverse_osmosis_or_nanofiltration rank lower despite strong removal performance

should_mention: impact of DOC competition on adsorption media life
spent media or resin handling requirements
